policy_statement: Expand production of high-value agroforestry products that help maintain healthy forests (e.g., sap/syrup, nuts, mushrooms, ginseng).
mechanism_description: The policy aims to expand production of high-value agroforestry products in ways that contribute to maintaining healthy forests, supporting ecosystem condition and carbon preservation.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem stewardship/land management → ↓ forest degradation/deforestation + maintained/increased biomass & soil carbon → ↑/preserved carbon stocks → ↓ net GHG emissions/forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Shift land-use and rural economic activity toward sustainable agroforestry product cultivation/harvest (e.g., nuts, syrup, mushrooms, medicinal plants) that depends on keeping forest canopy and structure intact, incentivizing long-term forest management and reduced conversion pressure.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved forest management and reduced disturbance maintain forest health (productivity, regeneration, canopy cover) and reduce degradation/clearing; continued tree growth and intact soils maintain or enhance carbon sequestration and storage in biomass and soils.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided CO₂ (and some N₂O/CH₄) emissions from land conversion/degradation and preservation (and potential increase) of terrestrial carbon sinks/stocks.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is maintaining or enhancing forest ecosystem condition so that forest carbon stocks are preserved (avoiding emissions from forest degradation/clearing and sustaining carbon storage).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem-focused land management that keeps forests intact/healthy, thereby preserving or increasing stored carbon and avoiding land-use change emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is climate mitigation (lower net emissions), the mechanism operates primarily through ecological systems and carbon sinks (forests), which is characteristic of Nature-Based Solutions rather than energy/resource efficiency measures.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “agroforestry products” expansion led to forest clearing to establish plantations, the mechanism would not preserve carbon; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly requires contributing to healthy forests/carbon preservation, keeping the dominant pathway nature-based.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism relies on forest-condition maintenance as the lever, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More granular subtypes (e.g., avoided deforestation vs. improved forest management vs. enhanced sequestration) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity if implementation incentivizes intensification or conversion (e.g., clearing understory/forest to “produce” agroforestry commodities) rather than stewardship; also possible minor livelihood/resilience co-benefits, but they do not dominate the climate pathway stated.
key_indicators: "Expand production"; "help to expand production"; "high-value agroforestry products"; "contribute to maintaining healthy forests"; "sap/syrup production, nuts, mushroom cultivation, and ginseng production"
co_benefits: Biodiversity habitat support; rural livelihoods/income diversification; non-timber forest product market development; potential watershed/soil health benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument details are not specified (could also be implemented as a planning-strategy or procurement-requirement), but the action-oriented phrasing (“should help to expand production”) most strongly implies incentives/assistance rather than regulation.
